metadata.title: Why Every Qubit Lives on a Globe

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: So a qubit's four real parameters shrink to two — normalization removes one degree of freedom and global phase removes another — leaving a single point on the Bloch sphere where the tilt from the north pole sets the probability of measuring zero. I want to make that map concrete. Help me: (1) derive the theta and phi parametrization directly from the amplitudes alpha and beta, (2) show where the plus state and the i state sit on the sphere, (3) explain how a single-qubit gate becomes a rotation of this globe.

props.segment: Why Every Qubit Lives on a

beats.narration_text: Every possible state of a quantum bit is a single point on one sphere — and the chance any measurement gives 'up' is just how far you've tilted from the top.

card.copy: Why Every Qubit Lives on a Globe

beats.narration_text: A qubit is a quantum system with two possible outcomes: zero and one, up and down. Its full state is a superposition: alpha times zero plus beta times one, where both are complex numbers. Quantum computers manipulate thousands of these simultaneously.

The Born rule says the probability of measuring zero is the squared modulus of alpha: a number between zero and one. For any measurement result the probabilities must sum to one. So the two complex numbers are tightly constrained.

The Question: a qubit's state has two complex numbers and seems to need four real parameters to describe it. Yet every qubit maps to a single point on a sphere, which takes only two. Where do the extra parameters go, and why a sphere?

card.copy: A qubit needs four real parameters — yet it maps to two. Where do the extras go, and why a sphere?

beats.narration_text: Two complex numbers should need four real parameters to fully specify. That suggests the space of qubit states is four-dimensional — a large, complicated space, not anything like a simple surface. A sphere is two-dimensional, so the map seems to throw away information.
Constraint one: normalization. The probabilities must sum to one, so the squared moduli of the two amplitudes add to one. That removes one degree of freedom — from four real parameters down to three.

Constraint two: global phase. If you multiply the entire state by any complex number of magnitude one — any phase — every probability you can measure is unchanged. That removes one more parameter. Three minus one equals two. And two real parameters label points on a sphere.

The two surviving parameters become a polar angle theta and an azimuth phi. Theta controls how much up versus how much down — at the north pole, theta equals zero, the qubit is all up. At the south pole, all down. The equator holds all equal superpositions.

The Born-rule probability becomes clean geometry. Place the state arrow and the analyzer arrow on the sphere. Let gamma be the angle between them. The probability of 'up' is cosine-squared of half that angle. Perpendicular arrows give fifty percent. Parallel gives one hundred. Antiparallel gives zero.

A physicist prepares a qubit at theta equals sixty degrees — two-thirds of the way from the north pole. She measures along the z-axis. The angle gamma equals sixty degrees. Probability of up equals cosine-squared of thirty degrees, which is three-quarters. She runs a thousand copies and gets about 750 up results.
beats.example_note: illustrative — qubit at theta=60 degrees, 1000 measurement trials

beats.narration_text: Why does every qubit live on a globe? Two complex amplitudes need four real parameters. Normalization removes one. Global phase removes another. Two remain — exactly two angles on a sphere. The Born-rule probability is just the cosine-squared of half the angle between state and analyzer.

card.copy: 4 real params - normalization - global phase = 2 = sphere. P(up) = cos^2(gamma/2).

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The Born-rule probability becomes clean geometry. Place the state arrow and the analyzer arrow on the sphere. Let gamma be the angle between them. The probability of 'up' is cosine-squared of half that angle. Perpendicular arrows give fifty percent. Parallel gives one hundred. Antiparallel gives zero. A physicist prepares a qubit at theta equals sixty degrees — two-thirds of the way from the north pole. She measures along the z-axis. The angle gamma equals sixty degrees. Probability of up equals cosine-squared of thirty degrees, which is three-quarters. She runs a thousand copies and gets about 750 up results.
